Proof of Theorem A The prototype of CY pairs adressed in Theorem A is ( Pn,D n+1), whereDn+1 is a hypersurface of degree n + 1 in Pn, n ≥ 3. The assumptions in particular say that Dn+1 is factorial, has terminal singularities, and Pic( Dn+1) = Cl(Dn+1) = ⟨ OPn(1)|Dn+1 ⟩ . Let us sketch the proof of Theorem A in this case. Suppose that ψ : ( Pn,D n+1)/ Spec C /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisright(X,D )/T is a volume-preserving birational map between Mf CY pairs that is not biregular. Then the ﬁrst step o f a Sarkisov factorisation of ψ is a divisorial contraction π : Y → Pn with centre Z ⊂ Pn. In Proposition 3.1, we show that Z ⊂ Dn+1 and codim Pn(Z) = 2. The assumption that Pic( Pn) → ClDn+1 is an isomorphism implies that Z is a complete intersection Z = Dn+1 ∩ Dd, where Dd ⊂ Pn is a hypersurface of degree d. Then we show in Lemma 3.2 that the cone of eﬀective divisors of Y is Eﬀ(Y ) = ⟨E, ˜Db⟩+ , where E denotes the exceptional divisor of π , b = min {d,n + 1}, and ˜Db denotes the strict transform of Db in Y . Therefore, the ﬁrst link in a Sarkisov factorisation of ψ is: Y Y ′ Pn W , π π ′ χ whereχ is a composition of Mori ﬂips, ﬂops and antiﬂips, and π ′: Y ′ → W is either a divisorial contraction or a Mori ﬁbre space that contracts the strict tr ansform of ˜Db. In any case, −KY ′ is π ′-ample. Since −KY ′ ∼ χ ∗ ˜Dn+1, we must have b = d < n+ 1, and π ′: Y ′ → W is a divisorial contraction with exceptional divisor ˜Db. A straightforward computation in the proof of Proposition 3.3 shows that in this case W has worse than terminal singularities, which is not allowed in a Sarkisov factorisation of ψ . This contradiction shows that the original map ψ : Pn /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisrightX is an isomorphism. Proposition 3.1. It follows from Theorem A that a very general quartic surface D ⊂ P3 satisﬁes Bir(P3,D ) = Aut( P3,D ). By very general we mean that D is smooth and satisﬁes the Lefschetz condition: Cl D = Z · OP3(1)|D. In this special case, a stronger result holds, and D can be taken to be only general. Indeed, to conclude that Bir( P3,D ) = Aut( P3,D ), it is enough to assume thatD is smooth and any curve of degree < 16 in D is a complete intersection of D with another surface in P3 (see [Tak98, Theorem 2.3 and Remark 2.4] and [Ogu13, Theorem 3.1]). 4. Extremal contractions In this section we classify extremal contractions of diﬀeren t types in various contexts. These results are the toolkit that we need to prove Theorems B and C. Indeed, the proofs of these results centre on the classiﬁcation of all possible volume p reserving Sarkisov links (X,D )/S /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisright(X †,D †)/S † that start from a known and given Mf CY pair ( X,D )/S . The links are themselves constituted of elementary modiﬁcations that are naturally associated to c ontractions of extremal rays: volume preserving extremal divisorial contractions; ﬂips, ﬂops, antiﬂips; and Mori ﬁber spaces. These elementary constituents are the topic of this section. The r esults are technical and we advise the reader to skip this section on ﬁrst reading and use it as refer ence, coming back to it as needed. 4.1. Divisorial contractions. Lemma 4.1. Let D ⊂ P3 be a quartic surface with canonical singularities, having e xactly one singular point z ∈ D. Let f : (Y,D Y ) → (P3,D ) be a volume preserving terminal divisorial contraction mapping a divisor E ⊂ Y to a closed point x ∈ P3. Then x =z ∈ D, and DY is the strict transform of D in Y . (1) If z is a singularity of D of type A1, then f is the blow-up of P3 at z. (2) If z is a singularity of D of type A2, choose homogeneous coordinates such that D is deﬁned by the equation x0x1x2 3 +x3B +C = 0 , where B = B3(x0,x 1,x 2) and C = C4(x0,x 1,x 2) are homogeneous polynomials of degree 3 and 4, respectively. Then either f is the blow-up of P3 at z, or f is the weighted blow-up of P3 atz with weights (2, 1, 1) or (1, 2, 1) — with respect to the aﬃne coordinates x0,x 1,x 2 in the open aﬃne chart (x3 = 1). Proof. By Proposition 3.1, x =z is the singular point of D. By Proposition 2.6, DY is the strict transform of D in Y . Choose homogeneous coordinates on P3 such that z = [0 : 0 : 0 : 1], and the equation of D has the form x2 3Q +x3B +C = 0 , whereQ,B,C ∈ C[x0,x 1,x 2] are homogeneous of degree 2 , 3 and 4, respectively. Then z ∈ D is anA1-singularity if and only if Q is a quadratic form of rank 3. If z ∈ D is anA2-singularity, then Q is a quadratic form of rank two, which — possibly after changi ng homogeneous coordinates — we may assume to be Q =x0x1. By [Kaw01, Theorem 1.1], in suitable analytic coordinates at z ∈ P3, the divisorial contraction f : Y → P3 is the weighted blow-up of z with weights (1,a,b ) where a andb are coprime integers. The diﬃculty in using this result is that at this point we do no t know that these analytic coordinates at z are induced from homogeneous coordinates on P3. Instead of using the result directly, we will use an equivalent statement that is coordi nate-free.20 CAROLINA ARAUJO, ALESSIO CORTI, AND ALEX MASSARENTI In general, suppose that z ∈ Z is a nonsingular point on a 3-fold Z, and that there are analytic coordinates at z ∈ Z such thatf : E ⊂ Y → z ∈ Z is the weighted blow-up with weights (1,a,b ), where 1 ≤ a< b and gcd(a,b ) = 1. The toric description of the weighted blow-up allows us to realize the valuation associated to E as follows. Construct inductively the tower of blow-ups: · · · →Zi → Zi− 1 → · · · → Z1 → Z0 =Z, where Zi → Zi− 1 is the blow-up of the centre z i− 1 = zEZi− 1 of the valuation E on Zi− 1. Note that Z1 → Z0 is the blow-up of z 0 = z. For every i, we denote by Ei ⊂ Zi the exceptional divisor, and for j >i we denote by Ej i ⊂ Zj the strict transform of Ei inZj. The following key properties follow directly from the toric description of th e weighted blow-up: (1) For all 0 ≤ j <a, the centre z j is a closed point of Zj. If j ≥ 1, then z j ∈ Ej ⊂ Zj, and if j ≥ 2, then zj ∈ Ej \Ej j− 1. (2) The centre z a ∈ Za is the generic point of a line La ⊂ Ea ∼ = P2. If a ≥ 2, then La ̸⊂ Ea a− 1. (3) For all a + 1 ≤ j <b, the centre z j ∈ Zj is a section Lj ⊂ Ej \Ej j− 1 of the projection Ej → Lj− 1. (4) Eb = E (by this we mean that the exceptional divisors Eb and E induce the same valuation on Z). We go back to our volume preserving terminal divisorial cont raction f : (Y,D Y ) → (P3,D ) mapping E ⊂ Y to z ∈ P3. Consider the tower above, starting with the blow-up σ1 : Z1 → Z0 = P3 of z 0 = z ∈ P3, and denote by Di ⊂ Zi the strict transform of D. We have that KZ1 =σ ∗ 1(KZ0) + 2E1 and D1 ∼ σ ∗ 1(D0) − 2E1, hence KZ1 +D1 =σ ∗ 1(KZ0 +D0). In other words, the birational morphism ( Z1,D 1) → (Z0,D 0) is volume preserving, and a(E,K Z1 +D1) = 0. This contradicts (i) and concludes the proof. □ 4.2. Extremal neighbourhoods. Deﬁnition 4.2. An extremal neighbourhood is the analytic germ of a 3-fold X around a pro- jective curve Γ ⊂ X. We say that the extremal neighbourhood is nonsingular if X is nonsingular. We say that a nonsingular extremal neighbourhood is trivial if it is isomorphic to the analytic germ around Γ in its normal bundle NΓ/X . In our situation, the curve Γ will always be contained in a giv en analytic germ of a surface S ⊂ X. Convention 4.3. An extremal neighbourhood is an analytic germ, that is, an eq uivalence class of analytic neighbourhoods (Γ ⊂ X), called representatives. Two representatives (Γ ⊂ X1) and (Γ ⊂ X2) are equivalent if there is a third representative contained in both: (Γ ⊂ X1) (Γ ⊂ X3) →↓ ↗↗qqqqqqqqqqq ←↓ ↘↘◆◆◆◆◆◆◆◆◆◆ (Γ ⊂ X2). When we speak of an extremal neighbourhood (Γ ⊂ X) we are often (and without saying so explicitly) abusing language and actually taking (Γ ⊂ X) to be a representative, which we may allow ourselves subsequently to shrink at our convenience. We often (and sometimes implicitly) assume that the extrema l neighbourhood is contractible, that is, that there is a projective morphism between suitabl e representatives π : (Γ ⊂ X) → (z ∈ Z), where (i) π is projective and Z is Stein (and hence, for any coherent sheaf F of OZ -modules, Hq(Z, F) = (0) ∀q >0); (ii) Γ = π − 1(z) and π maps X \Γ isomorphically to Z \ {z}. All of this is common practice when writing about germs, and w e trust that it will be clear from the context what is meant. Lemma 4.4. Consider a nonsingular extremal neighbourhood Γ ⊂ S ⊂ X, where S ∈ | − KX | is the germ of a nonsingular surface and Γ ∼ = P1. Assume that k =KX ·Γ = −S ·Γ ≥ 1. Then the following hold. (1) The extremal neighbourhood is trivial. (2) The antiﬂip X /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisrightX − exists, and X − has terminal singularities if and only if k = 1. Proof. To prove (1), we use [Mor82, Lemma 3.33], which in fact goes ba ck to [HR64, Lemma 9]. It is enough to show that the analytic germ around S ⊂ X is trivial, that is, X is analytically isomorphic to the analytic germ of S in its normal bundle NS/X . Indeed, S is the germ of a K3 surface, Γ is a ( − 2)-curve on S, and S itself is analytically isomorphic to the analytic germ of Γ in its normal bundle NΓ/S on S. First we check that the second inﬁnitesimal neighbourhood o f S in X is trivial. We denote by OS(1) the unique line bundle on S such that OS(1)|Γ = OΓ(1). The second inﬁnitesimal neighbourhood is an inﬁnitesimal extension of OS by OS(−S) = OS (k): 0 → O S(−S) → O 2S → O S → 0 ,22 CAROLINA ARAUJO, ALESSIO CORTI, AND ALEX MASSARENTI and these extensions are classiﬁed by H 1(S,T S (−S)) = H 1 ( OS(− 2 +k) ⊕ O S(2 +k) ) = 0. Now that we have shown that the second inﬁnitesimal neighbou rhood of S in X is trivial, we may apply [Mor82, Lemma 3.33], which states that the neighbo urhood of S ⊂ X is formally trivial if H 1( S,T X|S ⊗ O S(−nS) ) = 0 for all n ≥ 2. The latter statement in turn follows from the exact sequence 0 → TS → TX|S → O S(S) → 0 , giving H 1 ( S, OS (− 2 +nk) ⊕ O S(2 +nk) ) → H 1 ( S,T X|S ⊗ O S (−nS) ) → H 1 ( S, OS ( (n − 1)k )) for all n. We have shown that the neighbourhood of S in X is formally trivial. The neighbour- hood is analytically trivial by the main result of [HR64]. Now let us prove (2). F ano and weak F anoP1-bundles over P2. We state the classiﬁcation of rank two vector bundles E on P2 such that P(E) is Fano or weak Fano. We also collect some elementary facts and formulas on projective space bundles for later use. We begin by clarifying our conventions regarding vector bun dles. In this section, a vector bundle on a scheme is a locally free sheaf on it. Proof of Theorem B In this section we prove Theorem B. Consider a Mf CY pair ( P3,D ), where D ⊂ P3 is a quartic surface that is nonsingular outside a unique singul ar point z ∈ D of type A1, and such that the class group Cl( D) ∼ = Z is generated by the class of a hyperplane section. In this cas e, birational rigidity fails. The following Mf CY pair ( X,D X ) → P2 is a nontrivial element in P(P3,D ). Let σ : X → P3 be the blow-up of z, and DX ⊂ X the strict transform of D. It is a smooth K3 surface. The projection P3 /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisrightP2 fromz induces a Mori ﬁbration π : X → P2. One easily computes that KX +DX = σ ∗(KP3 +D). Theorem B states that the Mf CY pair ( X,D X ) → P2 is the only nontrivial element in the pliability set P(P3,D ). In the course of the proof, we will need the following result. Proposition 5.1. LetY be a smooth projective variety of dimension n,A a smooth semi-ample divisor on Y , and ϕ : Y → PN the morphism induced by the linear system |mA| for m ≫ 0. Suppose that: (i) the generic ﬁbre dimension of ϕ is ≤ n − 3; (ii) for all x ∈ PN , dim ( ϕ − 1(x) ) ≤ n − 2. Then the cokernel of the restriction homomorphism H 2(Y ; Z) → H 2(A; Z) is torsion-free. Proof. Set U =Y \A, and denote by i : U → Y andj : A → Y the inclusions. Consider the long exact sequence ... → H 2 c (U, Z) i∗ − → H 2(Y, Z) j∗ − → H 2(A, Z) → H 3 c (U, Z) → ... where Hi c(U, Z) denotes the singular cohomology with compact support. It i s enough to show that H 3 c (U, Z) is torsion free. By Poincar´ e duality, H 3 c (U, Z) ∼ =H2n− 3(U, Z). LetH ⊂ PN be the hyperplane such that ϕ ∗H =mA. Then ϕ restricts to a proper morphism ϕ U : U → PN \H. For each integer k, we denote by φ(k) be the dimension of the set of points y ∈ ϕ (U ) such that dim ( ϕ − 1(y) ) = k. If this set is empty, we set φ(k) = −∞ . By the main result of [GM88, Part II, Chap. 1, Sec. 1.1 ⋆] (Homotopy Dimension with Large Fibres), U has the homotopy type of a CW complex of real dimension less than o r equal to n + sup k { 2k − n +φ(k) + inf {φ(k), 0} } ≤ 2n − 3. — where the last inequality follows from the assumptions (i) and (ii). Finally, by [Nar67, Theorem 3], H2n− 3(U, Z) is torsion free. □ We are now ready to proceed with the proof of Theorem B. We star t by collecting a few useful facts on the geometry of DX.28 CAROLINA ARAUJO, ALESSIO CORTI, AND ALEX MASSARENTI 5.2 (The geometry of DX ). The surface DX ⊂ X intersects the exceptional divisor E ∼ = P2 of σ : X → P3 transversely along a smooth conic e. Denote by h the pull-back of a general hyperplane under σ|DX : DX → P3. Then Pic( DX ) = Z[h] ⊕ Z[e], and the intersection matrix of Pic(DX ) with respect to the basis ( [h], [e] ) is (5.3) ( 4 0 0 − 2 ) . The condition that Cl( D) ∼ = Z is generated by the class of a hyperplane section guarantees that D contains no lines, and so the restriction π|DX : DX → P2 is ﬁnite of degree 2. It ramiﬁes over a sextic curve. The associated involution τ : DX → DX maps the ( − 2)-curvee to another (− 2)-curvee′, the strict transform of the intersection of D with its tangent cone at z. Note that NE(DX ) = ⟨[e], [e′]⟩+, and e +e′ ∼ (π|DX )∗OP2(2) ∼ 2h − 2e. Thus, e′ ∼ 2h − 3e. Lemma 5.4. With the notation and assumptions of the preceding discussi on 5.2, we have Bir(DX ) = GD ⋊ ⟨τ⟩, whereGD ⊂ PGL4(C) is the group of projective automorphisms of D ⊂ P3. Proof. Since DX is the unique minimal model of D, it is follows that Bir( D) = Aut(DX ). Let us denote this group by G. It naturally acts on Pic( DX ) respecting the quadratic form and the Mori cone, thus we get a group homomorphism ρ : G → {± 1} where ρ(g) = 1 if ρ ﬁxes e and e′, and ρ(g) = − 1 if g exchanges e with e′. The involution τ exchanges e and e′, thus G = GD ⋊ ⟨τ⟩, where GD = Ker (ρ) is the subgroup of Aut( DX ) of elements that act trivially on Pic( DX ). Thus GD ﬁxes the class h ∈ Pic(DX ) and hence it acts on the linear system |OD(1)| as projective linear transformations. □ Remark 5.5. It is easy to see that if D is general then the group GD of projective automor- phisms of D ⊂ P3 is trivial. Let (X4,D 3, 4)/S be a Mf CY pair of the family of objects 4 described in the end of Section 2. The only volume preserving Sarkisov links Ψ : (X4,D 3, 4)/ Spec C /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisright(X †,D †)/S † are the maps (φa)− 1, (φb)− 1 described in Example 2.13. Proof. The 3-foldX4 has two singular points of type 1 2 (1, 1, 1) on the line P1 y0,y 1 ⊂ P(1, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2), and D3, 4 contains these two points as A1-singularities. By Proposition 3.1, either π (E) = Γ ⊂ D3, 4 is a curve, or π (E) = x ∈ D3, 4 is one of the two singular points. If π (E) = Γ ⊂ D3, 4 is a curve, then, by the main result [Kaw96], Γ avoids both the two singular points; and hence OD3,4(Γ) is a Cartier divisor. In the notation of § 6.2, D3, 4 is obtained from DX by contracting e0 and e′ 1. But OD3,4(C) = OD3,4(2) — for example because both line bundles have self-inters ection 12. Ifπ (E) = x ∈ D3, 4 is one of the two singular points, then — by the main result of [ Kaw96] — π is the weighted blow-up with weights (1 2, 1 2, 1 2 ) . The two extremal contractions give the links (φa)− 1 and (φb)− 1. □ Lemma 6.10. Let (X4,D 2, 4)/S be a Mf CY pair of the family of objects 5a described in the end of Section 2. The only volume preserving Sarkisov links Ψ : (X4,D 2, 4)/ Spec C /axisshort/axisshort/arrowaxisright(X †,D †)/S † are the maps (ψa)− 1, ( ˜ψb)− 1 described in Example 2.18. Proof. The key observation is this: X4 has a unique singular point [0 : 0 : 0 : 1 : 0] ∈ X4, analytically isomorphic to the germ at the origin of the hypersurface (6.11) xy +z3 +t3 = 044 CAROLINA ARAUJO, ALESSIO CORTI, AND ALEX MASSARENTI in C4. The surface D2, 4 passes through this point, and has an A2-singularity there. In fact, upon substituting y = −x1x3, one sees that the surface D2, 4 is the original surface D ⊂ P3. It follows from this that Cl( X4) → Cl(D2, 4) is an isomorphism. By Propositions 3.1 and 3.3, π : Z → X4 contracts the unique exceptional divisor to the singular point. By [Kaw03], up to isomorphism, there are pre cisely two divisorial contractions to a singular point as in Equation 6.11, given by the weighted blow-ups with weights (2, 1, 1, 1) and (1, 2, 1, 1).9 These two extremal contractions give the links ( ψa)− 1 and ( ˜ψb)− 1. As described there, the link terminates with π ′: F → P(14, 2), and it is clear from Equation 2.21 that π is the weighted blow up of the point x = [0 : 0 : 0 : 1 : 0] ∈ P(14, 2) with weights (1 , 1, 1, 2). The tangent cone of x ∈ X4 is y(y +x0 +x1), hence π induces the extremal divisorial contractions to x ∈ X4 where y has weight 2. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Develops a framework to describe birational geometry of Calabi–Yau (CY) pairs (X,D) via volume-preserving (crepant) birational maps; applies it to (P^3,D) with quartic D, showing how worsening singularities (A1, A2) enlarge Bir(P^3,D) and yield interesting subgroups of the 3D Cremona group. Proves: (A) rigidity/triviality of Bir(X,D) under terminality + class-group restriction (Cl D generated by restriction of a divisor on X) for Fano ρ=1; (B) complete description for quartic with one A1 singularity: pliability has exactly two Mf CY models, and Bir(P^3,D) fits a split exact sequence with kernel a torus form described by a Pell equation; (C) for one A2 singularity: pliability is infinite and explicitly listed (objects 1,2,3a,3b, a 3-parameter family 4, and a 7-parameter family 5a), connected by explicit volume-preserving Sarkisov links.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Uses volume-preserving (crepant) Sarkisov program (Corti–Kaloghiros) to factor any volume-preserving birational map into volume-preserving Sarkisov links between Mori-fibered CY pairs. Employs discrepancy/valuation characterizations of volume preservation, canonicity propagation, and detailed 3-fold MMP analysis: classification of terminal divisorial contractions (including weighted blow-ups) to smooth points and quotient/cDV singularities, analysis of extremal neighborhoods and (anti)flips, and explicit toric/GIT models for links. Computes Picard/class groups and Mori cones of K3 (resolutions/strict transforms of quartics) to constrain possible links; for Theorem B identifies kernel as identity component of orthogonal group of a binary quadratic form over C(x,y), yielding a G_m-twist (Pell-type group).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: No computational/empirical experiments. Validation is theoretical: (i) prove general rigidity via contradiction using first Sarkisov link and effective cone computations; (ii) in A1 case, analyze blow-up model X→P^3 and P^1-bundle π:X→P^2, control all possible subsequent links by studying extremal rays and classifying weak Fano P^1-bundles over P^2; compute exact sequence for Bir(P^3,D) using explicit involution and quadratic form invariants; (iii) in A2 case, construct explicit families of Mf CY pairs in weighted projective/GIT quotients and exhibit explicit coordinate formulas for Sarkisov links connecting them; then classify all possible links out of each object to prove completeness of the pliability list.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Results rely on characteristic 0 and on specific singularity types and class-group assumptions (quartic with exactly one A1 or A2 singularity, and Cl D = Z·O_D(1)); terminality/canonicity hypotheses are essential (Theorem A fails without them). Methods are heavily dimension-3 and use detailed classification of extremal contractions/neighborhoods; extension to higher dimensions or more complicated singularity configurations is nontrivial. For A2 case, output is an explicit but intricate list of models/links; group structure Bir(P^3,D) in A2 case is not fully presented as a compact algebraic description comparable to the A1 Pell-torus kernel. Dependence on ‘very general’ conditions for genericity statements about assumptions.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Extend the framework to other CY pairs (P^n, degree n+1 hypersurfaces) with controlled singularities and to other Fano 3-folds/ρ>1; classify Bir(X,D) and pliability for quartics with multiple or different ADE singularities, and for more singular/rational quartics. Seek a structural description (generators/relations) of Bir(P^3,D) in the A2 setting analogous to the A1 Pell-equation kernel, and study corresponding subgroups of the Cremona group. Develop more general ‘technology’ for explicit volume-preserving Sarkisov links in 3-fold Mf CY pairs (building on Section 4), possibly algorithmic/constructive classifications. Investigate interactions with automorphism groups of (resolved) K3 surfaces with higher Picard rank (e.g., Picard rank 2 cases) and with coregularity classifications; explore arithmetic/field-of-definition variants of the Pell-type torus forms.
